chapters.title: Our decisions constitute the core of traditions
chapters.text:
The 'things-have-always-been-this-way' approach is an integral attribute of traditions, yet most customs owe their popularity to certain people. For example, Americans generally believe that Dutch migrants brought Santa Claus to the United States. Although it's a creature of their folklore, it wasn't the Dutch who made Santa America's symbol for Christmas. Indeed, this celebration was typical for mid-17th-century Holland: Catholics closely associated Santa with St. Nicholas' Day. However, migrants to the New World were predominantly Protestants, who didn't approve of these customs.

A group of New Yorkers reinvented the Dutch tradition and made Santa a symbol of Christmas in the United States.

Therefore, people's decisions are the real driving force behind celebration rituals. For example, to publish a calendar marking the 25th of December as a holiday when no one else dared to; to return to nostalgic social harmony at Christmas, founding the New-York Historical Society. Or to popularize the unknown image of Santa by using its picture for advertisement in the press. In conducive circumstances, even the folklore base isn't essential: one family's habits can turn into a ritual beloved by the whole country.

Economic factors also played a critical part: it took a lot of time for Christmas to become the holiday we know today. We will see how agrarian and industrial societies transformed the meaning of the customs and their evaluation at the scale “acceptable — awkward.”

It is useful to think of traditions not as static entities but as dynamic forces that are constantly being negotiated and renegotiated. ~ Stephen Nissenbaum, PhD

Stephen Nissenbaum,

Come along as we span an unbelievable transformation of the most anticipated time of year. Our journey begins at a time when Americans almost stopped celebrating Christmas.

chapters.title: Puritans banned the celebration of Christmas, but it was a different holiday then
chapters.text:
In an agrarian society, life had a peculiar rhythm, full of arduous work in the field and at housekeeping. In December, when preparation for the winter was over, people could unwind and enjoy the outcomes of their efforts. Since wine or beer was already ready at that point, this month was well-known for its banquets.

Signs of the usual duration of celebration have survived to this day. Recall the carol “The Twelve Days of Christmas” or holiday Epiphany Eve, also known as Twelfth Night.

Alcohol often resulted in aggression or disorders, and at that time, people closely associated this excessive behavior with the Christmas season.

In New England, Puritans decided to root out these traditions since they didn't correspond to the meaning of the religious event. However, instead of encouraging people to master their unhealthy habits, they removed Christmas from calendars. In seventeenth-century New England, the latter lacked the marking of the 25th of December or any other holiday.

Furthermore, since 1659 those who celebrated this date had to pay five shillings, according to the law. On the one hand, it proved that many people continued banquets; otherwise, the law would be unnecessary. On the other hand, scientists didn't find any documents on prosecutions, so the number of Christmas keepers must have been small.

Yet one feature of these traditions made their elimination extremely difficult. Celebrating or not, everyone recognized that it was a special time. Therefore, the boundaries of accepted behavior broadened. The celebration resembled a carnival: men could dress in women's clothing and vice versa; many used masks. Kids mocked gentlemen, and apprentices imitated their masters. As we will soon see, this festive mood has made the season a unique opportunity to satisfy a critical need — restoring justice.

Did you know? The puritans even changed the names of weekdays to purify the almanacs. For example, they knew well that the fourth day indicated Thor, the god of thunder in Norse mythology.

chapters.title: Christmas carols weren't limited to best wishes initially
chapters.text:
The reversal of hierarchy was an essential component of carnival behavior. For instance, boys blockaded the school by guarding doors and windows to make the schoolmaster provide them with a holiday. The latter was reluctant to do it since his salary depended on working days, but he had no choice. Parents quietly approved of their kids' behavior.

Additionally, groups of youngsters, called wassailers, visited the houses of their patrons, singing. These songs contained threats in case the host refused to give the youth money or food. At the same time, the boys promised their goodwill and wished the best if the rich presented a gift to them. These best regards have remained in the carols until now.

The elite opened their houses to the poor. Even without this begging, prosperous families made gifts to their dependents. After all, the goodwill of peasants weighed a lot in an agrarian society. However, we shouldn't idolize that time. Periodically, youngsters could break the windows while singing. Gifts could permit wealthy people to mistreat their dependents during the year. Some slaveholders used presents as a tool of control: no obedience, no presents.

The rich of the seventeenth century interpreted this misrule as vital to maintaining the status quo. The elite believed it reinforced patriarchal relations, making the tradition entrenched. This tendency significantly strengthened when the puritans lessened the pressure. First, in 1681 the Restoration government in London forced them to cancel the ban on celebrating the holiday. John Tully even published his almanac, full of mentions of saints' days and, undoubtedly, Christmas. The return of the Puritans to power in 1689 kept celebrations on the side again. Yet this time, it didn't last long.

In 1712, the famous Puritan clergyman offered another approach in a sermon. It was a compromise: people could celebrate on the 25th of December but must avoid excessive drinking and other dishonorable behavior. The authors of almanacs quickly spread this idea, and a new interpretation of a celebration gradually gained popularity. A telling example: in 1758, authors still explained their choice to include this holiday, but not in 1760.

Mentioning Christmas in the calendar until 1730 was dangerous. Yet since 1760, all have marked the 25th of December in red ink.

Despite the popularity of almanacs, they weren't a decisive factor in transforming the celebration style. It was Santa Claus.

chapters.title: Clement Clarke Moore transformed Santa and added seven reindeer
chapters.text:
The history of Santa relates to the biography of civic leader John Pintard. He often recalled moments of harmony between different classes in his childhood. At the beginning of the eighteenth century, the situation was dramatically different. In the industrialization era, prosperous families didn't need goodwill anymore. Traditions of misrule have become twisted by revenge, often in hazardous ways. The wassailers transformed into groups of people who made noise near the houses of the rich on Christmas nights. No wonder the elite hardly celebrated on the 25th of December.

John Pintard also suffered from this misrule, being a wealthy merchant. Yet he decided to fight for the holiday. Thus, in 1804 he founded the New-York Historical Society and chose St. Nicholas to become its icon. He strived to make him the patron saint of the city. With his financial support, broadsides that contained a picture of St. Nicholas reached many people.

In 1819 another New-York Historical Society member enhanced the idea of peace between classes: a famous writer Washington Irving published a book that became an outstanding success. In stories dedicated to Christmas, the main characters discuss the harmony of old times. Along with Charles Dickens' books, these works created an image of the holiday we all know from the postcards. Yet implementation of imaginary traditions into real life took time.

Irving gave the elite an impetus to think about how to decrease the hostility of the poor. Soon Clement Clarke Moore found the solution. He created a poem, “A Visit from St. Nicholas,” and published it in 1823. It wasn't the first text about this saint: in 1821, an anonymous author created a story in which St. Nicholas used a sleigh and one reindeer to visit children at Christmas.

Moore added seven more reindeer and transformed the image of Santa. Instead of a bishop, kids met a small man in fur. His short pipe indicated that he belonged to the working class since the elite at that time used long ones. Unlike other versions, he never punished children, bringing them only gifts.

“A Visit from St. Nicholas” wasn't just a fairy tale for kids. It rethought the role of the poor: they did not come to request but to give.

chapters.title: Merchants used 1000-pound cakes, among other techniques, to draw consumers’ attention
chapters.text:
“A Visit from St. Nicholas” was a turning point as its success contributed to a new tradition — celebrating Christmas at home. It didn't overshadow other ways: bars continued to sell free alcohol on the 25th of December, so wassailing and drinking were ubiquitous. Still, Moore's poem had strong allies — newspapers. They popularized this idea, emphasizing that family values were crucial and represented misrule as a crime, which gradually made staying at home come into fashion.

Yet it wasn't merely a question of values. The family holiday entailed presents that people usually bought at Christmas. Therefore, merchants strived to have more clients, which needed safety measures to keep streets free from the aggressive crowd. The press was also involved in this business since the number of advertisements rocketed in December. All in all, the pressure on people to celebrate at home had some commercial roots.

Ads first appeared in newspapers along with the story about St. Nicholas. The target audience was primarily children; since 1809, gifts for women have also gained popularity. After 1820, the number of commercials surged; editors published supplements to post them. Soon the situation started to resemble a modern one. In 1834 journalists wrote that people had to meticulously plan their purchases to avoid falling into the traps of merchants that prompted customers to buy unnecessary things.

Giving into this temptation was highly likely since the variety of products and prices was immense: rings ranged from 25 cents to 25 dollars. Some shops created full-sized Santas or ads with him to draw kids' attention. Confectioners for this goal cooked “mammoth cakes” and competed with rivals in their weight: from 250 to almost 1000 pounds.

In the beginning, the most popular presents were books, and more than half of the ads were about them. From around 1826 to 1828, flyers contained 207 to over 1000 items. Gift books became bestsellers, containing tales, poetry, and marvelous illustrations. Each had a fascinating first page where someone could write a few kind words to make a present personal.

Market segmentation was astonishing at that time. There were gift books for readers of different political views, cultures, ages, and classes.

Personal Bibles were also trending in sales. Publishers even prepared illustrated ones to make them suitable presents for kids.

chapters.title: Literature significantly contributed to the popularity of the Christmas tree in the United States
chapters.text:
The Christmas tree is the final detail to make the celebration familiar to us. This custom spread in the United States in the mid-1830s. Legends related the origins of this tradition to Hessian soldiers or the husband of Queen Victoria with German roots. Yet these versions didn't coincide with historical facts, being too early or late. Even in Pennsylvania, the German community celebrated the 25th of December this way only in the 1810s. Why?

Although the Christmas tree is a German custom, the truth is more complicated. From the 17th century to 1750, this tradition was widespread only in Strasbourg. The turning point was in 1771 when a special guest, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, visited this city. He popularized Strasbourg's custom in his books. Yet the elite in Berlin accepted it only in the 1810s. After 20 years, this ritual finally transformed into a national practice.

Because of literature, Christmas trees became widespread in the United States and Germany simultaneously.

Information about the Christmas tree was diffused by means of commercial literature, not via immigrant folk culture—from the top down, not from the bottom up. ~ Stephen Nissenbaum, PhD

Stephen Nissenbaum,

Samuel Taylor Coleridge's stories about Christmas might have played a decisive role. They were published in the United States in 1824 and described a family celebration in Ratzeburg, a town in Germany. A small plot detail contributed largely to making this story a roaring success: children gave their handmade presents to their parents. Since Americans were anxious that their kids would become spoiled by the gifts, this gratitude deeply moved them. Coleridge's story was immediately in the books for children.

Catharine Sedgwick's “New Year's Day” (1835) made the Christmas tree a part of American literature.

Catharine Sedgwick represented a Christmas tree as a part of an American celebration, which was an essential change. Shortly after, publishers added her work to gift books and sold them in every corner.

Did you know? Catharine Sedgwick must have learned about the Christmas tree from Charles Folien, a Harvard professor and a German migrant. He was an active abolitionist and aimed to liberate the enslaved people. Charles Folien adhered to his principles even when many friends alienated him.

chapters.title: Dickens's “A Christmas Carol” set an example for the rich
chapters.text:
Presents to poorer people were an unresolved question for the rich. Should the elite give gifts to their workers? How should they treat beggars? Class issues were again on the agenda in terms of holiday celebrations. Charles Dickens's “A Christmas Carol” contained a hint for resolving this matter. At the end of the story, Ebenezer Scrooge donates a significant sum of money to charity, sends a turkey to the Cratchit family, and visits his nephew. Notably, he treated members of each class kindly but differently.

Dickens's story was one of the numerous stimuli rich people received to assist the poor. The press, charity organizations, and the elite encouraged others to donate. Besides, countless fiction stories reinforced that tendency: they spread the belief that helping children would lessen the class gap. As a result, Christmas dinners for impoverished children were a regular tradition, organized with patrons' donations.

Theodore Roosevelt's financial support helped arrange Christmas dinners from 1870 to 1873.

If prosperous families strived to give presents personally, charity organizations chose well-behaved kids for them. Moreover, during large festive meals, the rich could visit them and observe how thousands of poor children were eating. Before the dinner, impoverished and wealthy people sang a hymn together. Reporters interpreted it as the disappearance of a gap between classes at Christmas.

Some of the elite did it because they rarely saw the same joy at home. No matter how much money they spent on gifts, it was more challenging to surprise their children each year. It was especially harmful to women, who organized the home celebration, putting all their efforts not to let relatives down, but had no gratitude. Unsurprisingly, after Christmas, the rate of illnesses among women rocketed.

Whatever the motives of wealthy families were, it was quite an ambiguous ritual, and some of the poor protested. Youngsters, especially boys who sold cheap newspapers, threw spoons, forks, and mince pies. It didn't mean the boys weren't hungry, but they surely didn't appreciate being watched.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The celebration of Christmas reflects the historical context in which it takes place. In the beginning, it was a season to recharge the batteries or break bad, drinking and making a fuss. People turned the hierarchy upside down, and the poor stood a chance to receive gifts from their sovereigns. In return, they promised their goodwill.

Industrialization and commercialization made these traditions obsolete. Soon with the help of certain people, the 25th of December gained a new meaning and customs. It became a family holiday with presents for children, letters for Santa Claus, and a celebration near a Christmas tree.

Some argue that writers, reporters, and publishers played a crucial role in creating a new image of the holiday, yet it's only a part of the truth. Often, they only described what they saw; for example, the children, who moved Coleridge and many Americans of the eighteenth century, were also crucial.

This idea leads us to an essential observation: we all generate customs. Thus, it's up to us to implement a change that would become our family's tradition.

Try this:


• Create an inspirational playlist with tracks that bring good memories and a festive mood. It could be songs about Christmas or others you listen to during the holidays.
• ‌Ask your relatives about how they celebrated this special night in childhood. What is their favorite tradition? Is there something they want to return?
• ‌Think about a custom that can make this Christmas unforgettable. It could be a surprise or unusual activity.
